text.title: Guardrails Concepts Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules for guardrail architecture selection.

rule.statement: Every pipeline stage receives at least one rail: input rail before LLM call, output rail before user, retrieval rail between vector search and LLM (RAG), dialog rail across turns (multi-turn), execution rail before tool call (agent).

rationale.source: NeMo Guardrails docs + Guardrails AI architecture

rule.rationale: Stages without rails are unmitigated attack surfaces. Per-stage rails are independent — input rail alone cannot catch unsafe outputs and output rail alone cannot prevent injection.

rule.statement: Embedded guardrails (RLHF, Llama Guard classification) cover general safety; programmable guardrails (NeMo, Guardrails AI, custom validators) cover business-specific rules — never substitute one for the other.
rule.rationale: Embedded rails are baked into the model and add zero latency but cannot be tuned per-request; programmable rails are auditable and domain-specific but cost latency. Both are required for a defensible posture.

rule.statement: Each rail declares one of five actions on violation: block (no safe fallback), filter (remove fragment, keep context), transform (mask / rephrase), warn (flag for human review), log (record only). Action choice follows severity, not convenience.
rule.rationale: Universal-block is the lazy default and destroys UX; universal-warn is the lazy default and creates compliance debt. Per-violation action mapping is the only sustainable policy.

rule.statement: Run checks in tiered order: regex / pattern match first (under 1 ms), fast classifier second (10-50 ms), LLM-as-judge only for borderline cases (500 ms+). Stop at the first tier that fires a violation.

rationale.source: Guardrails AI benchmarks; NeMo perf notes

rule.rationale: LLM-as-judge is most accurate but breaks any production latency budget at 100% application. Tiered evaluation cuts median guardrail latency 10-100x while preserving recall.

rule.statement: Prompt-injection defence requires four layers: input sanitisation, instruction hierarchy (system > user > tool output), output validation (instruction-echo check), and isolation (user content wrapped in delimiters). One layer alone is insufficient.

rationale.source: OWASP LLM Top 10 2026; OpenAI injection postmortem

rule.rationale: Single-layer defences are defeated by known attack chains within weeks of disclosure. Four layers create combinatorial cost for the attacker.

rule.statement: Logs store sanitised content + metadata only: timestamp, request_id, guardrail_type, result (pass/fail/warn), latency_ms, violation_details, hashed user_id. Never log raw user input or raw LLM output.
rule.rationale: Raw logs become a new PII surface and a compliance liability. Structured metadata gives all signal needed for tuning and incident response without the regulatory cost.
